abstract.text: In the standard cosmological model, the merger remnant of the Milky Way and Andromeda (Milkomeda) will be the only galaxy remaining within our event horizon once the Universe has aged by another factor of ten, ~10^{11} years after the Big Bang. After that time, the only extragalactic sources of light in the observable cosmic volume will be hypervelocity stars being ejected continuously from Milkomeda. Spectroscopic detection of the velocity-distance relation or the evolution in the Doppler shifts of these stars will allow a precise measurement of the vacuum mass density as well as the local matter distribution. Already in the near future, the next generation of large telescopes will allow photometric detection of individual stars out to the edge of the Local Group, and may target the ~10^{5+-1} hypervelocity stars that originated in it as cosmological tracers.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper proposes that hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the future merged galaxy Milkomeda will serve as cosmological tracers, enabling future observers to measure the vacuum energy density (ρ_v) and the local mass distribution via Doppler shift and luminosity-based distance measurements. The radial orbits of these stars are governed by a balance between galactic gravity and the cosmological constant, with the transition radius R_tran ≈ (GM_tot/H_v²)^(1/3) marking where vacuum dominance takes over; spectroscopic detection of velocity-distance relations beyond this radius allows reconstruction of ρ_v, while closer stars reveal the internal mass distribution of Milkomeda.

source_fields.journal_name: Journal of Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics
source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions.: In the distant future, hypervelocity stars from Milkomeda will serve as cosmic probes to empirically validate the standard cosmological model, including the cosmological constant and early universe conditions, through precise measurements of their Doppler shifts and light element abundances in metal-poor stars. Introduction.: The novel contribution is that hypervelocity stars escaping the merging Milkomeda galaxy will enable future observers to measure the vacuum energy density and validate the standard cosmological model despite the eventual loss of observational access to external galaxies and the cosmic microwave background. Cosmology with Hypervelocity Stars.: This section proposes that hypervelocity stars, naturally ejected from the galactic center, can be used as cosmic probes to measure the cosmological constant and the mass distribution of galaxies like Milkomeda by analyzing their Doppler shifts and distances as they travel beyond the transition radius where dark energy dominates.

title: Cosmology with Hypervelocity Stars

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical paper proposing future use of hypervelocity stars as cosmological tracers; no observational data or catalog presented
